Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: We are given an event log for a customer support ticket resolution process We need to identify cases with significantly longer total resolution times determine potential root causes and propose insights recommendations

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First lets compute the total resolution time for each case The resolution time is from the first event Receive Ticket to the last event Close Ticket

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 101 Start 2024 03 01 08 00 End 2024 03 01 10 15 Duration 2 hours 15 minutes 2 25 hours

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 102 Start 2024 03 01 08 05 End 2024 03 02 09 15 total 25 hours 10 minutes 25 1667 hours

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 103 Start 2024 03 01 08 10 End 2024 03 01 09 30 Duration 1 hour 20 minutes 1 333 hours

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 104 Start 2024 03 01 08 20 End 2024 03 02 08 30 total 24 hours 10 minutes 24 1667 hours

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 105 Start 2024 03 01 08 25 End 2024 03 03 09 30 total 49 hours 5 minutes 49 0833 hours

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Clearly cases 102 104 and 105 have much longer resolution times compared to 101 and 103 Cases 102 and 104 take about a day and case 105 takes about two days So these are the significantly longer ones

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Now we need to determine potential root causes We should look at the event sequences and timestamps to identify where delays occur

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 102 Receive 08 05 Triage 08 30 Assign to L1 09 00 Escalate to L2 11 30 Investigate Issue 14 00 Resolve next day 09 00

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Possibly the escalation caused a handoff delay

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 104 Receive 08 20 Triage 09 00 Assign to L1 09 30 Investigate Issue 13 00 Resolve next day 08 00

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Case 105 Receive 08 25 Triage 08 35 Assign to L1 09 00 Investigate Issue 09 10 Escalate to L2 10 00 Investigate Issue next day 14 00 Resolve day3 09 00

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Possibly the Level 2 team has a backlog or the ticket waited in a queue

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Compare with the shorter cases Case 101 and 103 have relatively quick transitions between activities and no escalations

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Escalations appear in cases 102 and 105 and both have long resolution times However case 104 does not have an escalation but still has a long resolution time So escalation is not the only factor

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Long waiting times between activities In case 102 there is a 2 point 5 hour wait between Assign and Escalate and another 2 point 5 hour wait between Escalate and Investigate In case 104 there is a 3 point 5 hour wait between Assign and Investigate In case 105 there is a huge gap of 28 hours before the next Investigate after escalation

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Tickets that are not resolved within the same business day incur overnight delays which significantly increase cycle time

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: In case 104 there is no escalation but there is a long delay between Assign and Investigate and then from Investigate to Resolve Possibly the Level 1 agent took a long time to start investigation after assignment or there was a queue

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Potential root causes Escalations introduce handoff delays and potentially longer processing times if the specialized team is busy Long waiting times between activities especially before investigation indicate possible bottlenecks in agent availability or prioritization Tickets that are not resolved within the same business day incur overnight delays Possibly tickets that require more complex investigation are prone to delays

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The longest delays are in case 105 28 hours between Escalate and second Investigate and 19 hours between second Investigate and Resolve In case 102 150 minutes between Assign and Escalate 150 minutes between Escalate and Investigate and 19 hours between Investigate and Resolve In case 104 210 minutes between Assign and Investigate and 19 hours between Investigate and Resolve

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: It seems that if a ticket is not resolved by the end of the business day it gets postponed to the next day adding roughly 15 to 16 hours of non working time This is a major factor in long cycle times

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Escalations significantly increase resolution time not only due to additional processing but also due to handoff delays and possible queueing for specialized agents Long waiting times between assignment and investigation indicate that Level 1 agents might be overloaded Tickets that are not resolved within the same business day incur substantial overnight delays

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: Recommendations Monitor and reduce handoff times between teams Ensure Level 2 agents have clear service level agreements Analyze workload distribution among Level 1 agents Implement policies to encourage resolution within the same business day Consider extending support hours or having overlapping shifts For tickets that require escalation have Level 1 agents gather more information upfront Use process mining to identify bottlenecks
